claim: Model Context Protocol (MCP) recently deprecated the Resource API in favor of tools; most MCP servers now use tools instead of resources
actionable: When building MCP servers, use tools instead of resources; tools provide the same functionality with simpler semantics
source_title: Agent Skills or MCP in the era of Claude Code?

claim: MCP now supports streamable HTTP alongside Server-Sent Events, making cloud-deployed MCP servers easier to network
actionable: Deploy MCP servers to the cloud with streamable HTTP; avoids networking pain points of SSE for remote agents
source_title: Agent Skills or MCP in the era of Claude Code?

claim: MCP OAuth 2.1 support enables agents to authenticate to backend systems on behalf of the user; designed for locally-running agents with browser OAuth flows
actionable: Use OAuth 2.1 MCP for cloud-based backend authentication; users see standard OAuth dialogs; credentials stay in MCP server, not agent
source_title: Agent Skills or MCP in the era of Claude Code?

claim: Skills and MCP overlap for local coding agents but remain distinct: skills are local markdown files with static resources; MCP provides real-time data and cloud authentication
actionable: Use skills for local reference data and lightweight code scripts; use MCP for real-time data, authentication, and microservice integration
source_title: Agent Skills or MCP in the era of Claude Code?

claim: Agentic microservices still require MCP because skills do not scale to multi-agent systems or cloud deployments; CLI tools with auth work for local agents but not remote services
actionable: For microservices/multi-agent systems, use MCP; do not rely on skills or CLI tools for cross-service communication
source_title: Agent Skills or MCP in the era of Claude Code?
